title: Your fine-tuned model is worse than the base model
problem: You fine-tuned gemini-2.5-flash on 500 customer support examples. The fine-tuned model scores lower on your eval set than the base model with a well-crafted system prompt. List four specific data quality issues that could cause this, explain how each one degrades the model, and describe how you would audit your training data to find them.
hints: Inconsistency: what happens if the same question has three different answer styles across examples?
Label leakage: what if your 'gold' training responses were themselves generated by the base model?
Coverage: 500 examples — is that enough for the variety of intents in production?
solution: Four data quality issues: (1) Inconsistency — if the same intent is answered in three different tones across examples, the model averages them and produces bland, hedged output. Audit: cluster examples by intent and check variance in response style. (2) Label leakage / circular training — if training responses were generated by the base model, fine-tuning reinforces its existing biases rather than learning from human judgement. Audit: check provenance of every training response. (3) Insufficient coverage — 500 examples spread across 20 intents is only 25 per intent; the model memorises rather than generalises. Audit: count examples per intent class; aim for 100+ per class. (4) Length bias — if gold responses are all short, the model learns to be terse even when a detailed answer is needed. Audit: plot response length distribution and compare to production query complexity. Fix: curate a smaller, higher-quality dataset rather than adding more low-quality examples.
